title: Retirement Calculator for Colorado Residents: 2026 Guide
metaDescription: Free retirement calculator for Colorado residents. Account for 4.4% top tax rate, Social Security taxation, COL index 107, and PERA in your Colorado retirement plan.

content.introduction:
Retirement planning in Colorado isn't just about how much you save — it's about how much you keep. The Centennial State has a flat income tax with rates up to 4.4%, which directly impacts your retirement withdrawals. With a cost of living index of 107 (100 = national average), your retirement dollar goes about as far as the national average.

A retirement calculator for colorado residents needs to factor in these state-specific variables — not just generic 7% returns and 4% withdrawal rates. This guide covers exactly how Colorado's tax structure, pension systems, Social Security treatment, and cost of living change your retirement math, with worked examples using real Colorado numbers.

sections.heading: Colorado's Retirement Tax Landscape
sections.content:
Colorado taxes retirement income at rates up to 4.4%, and also taxes Social Security benefits. Colorado taxes Social Security for residents under 65. At 65+, up to $20,000 excluded ($24,000 at 65+). Full exclusion for those born before 1938.

Key tax factors for Colorado retirees:

  • Income Tax: Flat 4.4% on federal taxable income (reduced from 4.55% in 2024)
  • Social Security: Colorado taxes Social Security for residents under 65. At 65+, up to $20,000 excluded ($24,000 at 65+). Full exclusion for those born before 1938.
  • Estate/Inheritance Tax: No state estate or inheritance tax.
  • Senior Property Tax Relief: Senior Homestead Exemption: 50% of first $200,000 assessed value for 10+ year owner-occupants age 65+.

On a $40,000 annual withdrawal, Colorado retirees could owe up to $1,760 in state income tax — a meaningful drag on retirement income. Use our retirement calculator to see how this affects your specific numbers.

sections.heading: How Much Do You Need to Retire in Colorado?
sections.content:
The answer depends on your lifestyle, but here's the math using Colorado-specific cost data:

  • Median household income: $82,254
  • Cost of living index: 107 (near national average)
  • Target retirement income: 80% of pre-retirement income = $70,409/year
  • Portfolio needed (4% rule): $1,760,225

The 4% rule means withdrawing 4% of your portfolio annually, adjusted for inflation. For Colorado residents earning the median income, you'd need roughly $1,760,225 invested at retirement to maintain your current lifestyle — plus an additional buffer for state income taxes of up to 4.4%.

Social Security replaces roughly 40% of pre-retirement income for average earners, potentially reducing your needed portfolio to around $1,056,135.

sections.heading: PERA: State Pension Benefits
sections.content:
Colorado's public pension system — PERA — plays a significant role for public employees. Colorado Public Employees Retirement Association. Defined benefit with 2024 reforms increasing member contributions. Well-funded but underwent restructuring.

If you're a Colorado public employee, your retirement calculator inputs change significantly:

  • Pension income: Typically replaces 50-70% of final average salary for career employees
  • Social Security interaction: Some Colorado public employees don't pay into Social Security — your pension is the primary replacement
  • Supplemental savings: Even with a pension, most financial planners recommend building a 457(b) or supplemental retirement account for flexibility

For private-sector workers, your retirement income comes entirely from personal savings (401(k), IRA, taxable accounts) plus Social Security. The retirement calculator helps you model exactly how much to save each month.

sections.heading: Retirement Savings Timeline for Colorado Workers
sections.content:
Using Colorado's median household income of $82,254 and a 15% savings rate:

Start AgeMonthly SavingsPortfolio at 65Monthly Income (4% rule)
25$1,028$2,698,308$8,994
30$1,028$1,851,484$6,172
35$1,028$1,254,130$4,180
40$1,028$832,754$2,776
45$1,028$535,513$1,785

Starting at 25 vs 35 nearly doubles your retirement portfolio — that's the power of compound interest over an extra decade. Every year of delay costs approximately $96,430 in final portfolio value.

Explore different timelines with our compound interest calculator to see exactly how compounding works for your situation.

sections.heading: Colorado Cost of Living Impact on Retirement
sections.content:
Colorado's cost of living index of 107 means retirees here need 7% more than the national average to maintain the same lifestyle. Housing, healthcare, and everyday expenses run higher in the Centennial State.

What this means for your retirement number:

  • National benchmark: $1 million portfolio → $40,000/year (4% rule)
  • Adjusted for Colorado: $1 million portfolio → effectively $37,383/year in purchasing power
  • Colorado-adjusted target: To match $40,000 national purchasing power, you need $1,070,000

Colorado's costs are manageable for well-planned retirees.

sections.heading: Tax-Advantaged Accounts: Maximize Your Colorado Savings
sections.content:
Regardless of where you live, maximize these accounts before taxable investing:

  • 401(k)/403(b): $24,000 limit in 2026 ($32,000 if 50+) — employer match is free money
  • Traditional IRA: $7,500 limit ($8,500 if 50+) — tax-deductible, reducing your Colorado state tax bill
  • Roth IRA: $7,500 limit — grows tax-free forever. Particularly valuable in Colorado: you skip the 4.4% state tax on all future withdrawals.
  • HSA: $4,350 individual / $8,750 family — triple tax advantage for healthcare costs in retirement

529 Plans: Full deduction of contributions to any state 529 plan (CollegeInvest or others) against CO income. No cap.

For Colorado residents, the Roth IRA is especially compelling: paying 4.4% state tax now to avoid it on decades of growth and withdrawals often makes mathematical sense.

sections.heading: Common Retirement Planning Mistakes in Colorado
sections.content:
• Ignoring state tax changes: Colorado's tax rates can change. Plan for current rates but build flexibility into your strategy.
  • Underestimating healthcare costs: The average 65-year-old couple needs $315,000+ for healthcare in retirement, and this figure applies nationally regardless of COL
  • Forgetting inflation: At 3% inflation, $40,000/year buying power shrinks to $24,000 in 15 years. Your calculator should model inflation-adjusted withdrawals.
  • Over-relying on Social Security: The average Social Security benefit is ~$1,900/month. For Colorado residents, that covers only 32% of estimated expenses.
  • Not accounting for Colorado-specific costs: Wildfire insurance, earthquake preparedness, and water costs can surprise Colorado retirees.

content.practicalExample:
Retirement Scenario: Colorado Worker, Age 35

Profile: A 35-year-old Colorado resident earning $82,254/year, saving 15% for retirement.

Inputs:

  • Monthly contribution: $1,028
  • Years to retirement: 30
  • Expected return: 7% (historical stock market average)
  • Current savings: $50,000

Results at Age 65:

  • Portfolio value: $1,254,130
  • Annual withdrawal (4% rule): $50,165
  • Monthly retirement income: $4,180
  • Est. state tax on withdrawals: -$92/month (effective rate ~2%)
  • Plus Social Security (~$1,900/month at full retirement age)

Bottom line: Combined retirement income of ~$6,080/month before state taxes — sufficient to cover estimated expenses.

Model your own scenario with our retirement calculator.
content.conclusion:
Retirement planning for Colorado residents requires more than plugging numbers into a generic calculator. The Centennial State's 4.4% top income tax rate, Social Security taxation, cost of living index of 107, and pension system (PERA) all materially change how much you need and how long your savings will last.

Start with our retirement calculator to model your specific situation — input your actual income, savings rate, and expected retirement age to see where you stand. Then use the compound interest calculator to visualize how starting today versus next year impacts your final number.

faqs.question: Does Colorado tax Social Security benefits?
faqs.answer: Colorado taxes Social Security for residents under 65. At 65+, up to $20,000 excluded ($24,000 at 65+). Full exclusion for those born before 1938. This means Social Security recipients in Colorado should factor state taxes into their retirement income projections.

faqs.question: What is a good retirement savings target for Colorado?
faqs.answer: Based on Colorado's cost of living index of 107 and median income of $82,254, a household targeting 80% income replacement needs approximately $1,760,225 in retirement savings (using the 4% rule). Social Security reduces this by roughly 40%, bringing the savings target to about $1,056,135. Adjust based on your actual expenses, healthcare needs, and desired lifestyle.

faqs.question: Is Colorado a good state to retire in?
faqs.answer: Colorado has a 4.4% top tax rate on retirement income, which is moderate and offset by other benefits. The cost of living index of 107 means your money goes less far than the national average. Consider: Senior Homestead Exemption: 50% of first $200,000 assessed value for 10+ year owner-occupants age 65+.

faqs.question: How does PERA work?
faqs.answer: Colorado Public Employees Retirement Association. Defined benefit with 2024 reforms increasing member contributions. Well-funded but underwent restructuring. Colorado's pension system covers public employees including teachers, state workers, and often local government employees. Benefits are typically calculated as a formula: years of service multiplied by a percentage (often 1.5-2.5%) multiplied by final average salary. Contact PERA directly for your specific benefit estimate, and use our retirement calculator to model how pension income supplements your personal savings.
